description:
You are helping a colleague who is drafting a tiny **user-level systemd “prototype service”** as part of a utilities bundle.  
  Your goal is to put everything in place so the colleague can later enable the service with a single command.

  1. Create the directory “/home/user/scripts” if it does not already exist.
  2. Inside that directory write an executable Bourne-shell script called “hello_time.sh”.  
     • It must start with the she-bang “#!/usr/bin/env bash” (no spaces in front).  
     • The one and only command in the body has to append a UTC time-stamp, followed by the literal string “ - hello”, to a log file in the user’s home directory:  
       ``echo "$(date -u +'%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ') - hello" >> /home/user/hello_time.log``  
       (Keep the double quotes and the Z suffix exactly; automated tests rely on this.)
  3. Make the script executable for the owner (agent may use any standard method).
  4. Place a user-level systemd unit file at  
     “/home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-time.service”  
     with exactly the following three sections and keys (no extra keys, no reordering):  

     [Unit]  
     Description=Hello Time Logger  

     [Service]  
     Type=oneshot  
     ExecStart=/home/user/scripts/hello_time.sh  

     [Install]  
     WantedBy=default.target  

     The automated tests will match the file *verbatim*, so spelling, capitalisation and spacing must be identical to the above block.

  5. Finally, execute the script manually **once** so that the log file “/home/user/hello_time.log” gets its first entry.

  Log-file requirements (strictly enforced by tests):  
  • Path: /home/user/hello_time.log  
  • After you run the script there must be exactly one line in the file.  
  • The line must follow this precise format (example shown):  
    2024-05-27T14:05:33Z - hello  
    ‑ Four-digit year, two-digit month, etc., “T” separator, “Z” suffix, a single space, a hyphen, another space, the word “hello”.  
  • No leading/trailing spaces, no newline after the final newline (a single “\n” is fine).  

  Summary of artefacts the grader will look for:
  • /home/user/scripts/hello_time.sh → correct she-bang line, correct single echo line, executable bit set.  
  • /home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-time.service → content must match the block above exactly.  
  • /home/user/hello_time.log → contains one and only one correctly formatted line.

  You do **not** need to enable or start the unit with systemctl; just create the files and run the script once.
truth:
Expected final state:

  1. File: /home/user/scripts/hello_time.sh  
     Permissions: owner executable (any mode where owner can execute, e.g., 700 or 755).  
     Exact content (including trailing newline):  
     ```
#!/usr/bin/env bash
echo "$(date -u +'%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ') - hello" >> /home/user/hello_time.log
```

  2. File: /home/user/.config/systemd/user/hello-time.service  
     Permissions: default user-created file perms (e.g., 644).  
     Exact content (including trailing newline):  
     ```
[Unit]
Description=Hello Time Logger

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/home/user/scripts/hello_time.sh

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

  3. File: /home/user/hello_time.log  
     • Exactly one line present.  
     • Regex used by grader (anchored, newline allowed at end):  
       ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z - hello\n?$  

  Pre-existing environment: only the default empty /home/user.  No scripts, log file or systemd directories exist prior to task start.
